If Codex is unavailable but Copilot is available, use Copilot only for fresh-session roles:\n   124\t   - story implementor: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   125\t   - quick fixer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`\n   126\t   - story verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   127\t   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   128\t   - epic verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   129\t   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   130\t   - epic synthesizer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   131\t   Story implementor still falls back to `secondary_harness: \"none\"` because v1 retained implementor continuation is not defined for Copilot.\n   132\t5. If no GPT secondary harness is available, switch all roles to `secondary_harness: \"none\"` and record a degraded-diversity condition in `team-impl-log.md`.\n   133\t\n   134\tThis keeps config authorship with the orchestrator while still satisfying the epic’s requirement that defaults and fallback behavior be resolved deterministically before implementation begins.\n   135\t\n   136\t### `team-impl-log.md`\n   137\t\n   138\t`team-impl-log.md` is the durable orchestration record. It is the primary run-state artifact, not a narrative afterthought. The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.\n   139\t\n   140\tRequired sections:\n   141\t\n   142\t| Section | Purpose |\n   143\t|---|---|\n   144\t| Run Overview | spec-pack root, current status, current story, current phase |\n   145\t| Run Configuration | snapshot of the validated `impl-run.config.json` choices |\n   146\t| Verification Gates | story and epic gate commands discovered from project policy |\n   147\t| Story Sequence | full story list and current position |\n   148\t| Receipts | pre-acceptance evidence and dispositions by story |\n   149\t| Cumulative Baselines | test-count expectations and regression checks |\n   150\t| Cleanup / Epic Verification | deferred items, cleanup status, final verification state |\n   151\t| Open Risks / Accepted Risks | explicit unresolved or accepted-risk items |\n   152\t\n   153\tThe log remains markdown because it is both human-facing and agent-facing. The orchestrator needs to read and update it directly. A second support file can be added later if highly structured state becomes necessary, but the v1 feature should keep the required surface small.\n   154\t\n   155\t### State Enum\n   156\t\n   157\tThe log should carry one explicit current-state field using this enum:\n   158\t\n   159\t| State | Meaning |\n   160\t|---|---|\n   161\t| `SETUP` | skill loaded, spec-pack root being resolved, config/log initialization not complete |\n   162\t| `BETWEEN_STORIES` | ready to start the next story |\n   163\t| `STORY_ACTIVE` | implementation / verification / fix routing in progress for one story |\n   164\t| `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY` | all stories accepted, cleanup and epic closeout not yet complete |\n   165\t| `EPIC_VERIFY_ACTIVE` | cleanup, epic verifier batch, or synthesis is in progress |\n   166\t| `COMPLETE` | run complete |\n   167\t| `FAILED` | blocked or failed terminally pending human decision |\n   168\t\n   169\t### `team-impl-log.md` Template\n   170\t\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-08/006-verifier-codex53-high.jsonl:6:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_1","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/stories/08-epic-verification-synthesis-and-closeout.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Story 8: Epic Verification, Synthesis, and Closeout\n\n### Summary\n<!-- Jira: Summary field -->\nMaterialize cleanup work, run epic-level verification and synthesis, and keep the final epic gate with the orchestrator before closeout.\n\n### Description\n<!-- Jira: Description field -->\n\n**User Profile**\n\n- **Primary User:** A Claude Code orchestration agent operating on behalf of a senior engineer or tech lead\n- **Context:** Running a completed spec pack through a disciplined implementation workflow without relying on conversation memory or ad hoc orchestration\n- **Mental Model:** \"I load a finished spec pack, confirm the environment and options, launch the right workflow through the CLI, and supervise implementation and verification until the epic is complete.\"\n- **Key Constraint:** Claude Code is always available. GPT model lanes depend on whether Codex CLI or Copilot CLI is available in the local environment.\n\n**Objective**\n\nAfter all stories are accepted, compile deferred and accepted-risk items into a cleanup artifact, review and execute the approved cleanup batch, run fresh epic-level verification, run mandatory synthesis, and leave the final epic gate with the orchestrator before closeout.\n\n**In Scope**\n\n- Pre-epic cleanup artifact and cleanup review flow\n- Cleanup execution and verified cleaned state\n- Epic verifier batch\n- Mandatory epic synthesis\n- Orchestrator-owned final epic gate\n\n**Out of Scope**\n\n- Story implementation\n- Story verification and quick-fix loops\n- Initial run setup and provider default resolution\n\n**Dependencies**\n\n- Story 7 complete\n- All prior stories accepted\n- Cleanup artifact materialized from prior story cycles\n\n### Acceptance Criteria\n<!-- Jira: Acceptance Criteria field -->\n\n**AC-7.1:** Deferred and accepted-risk items are materialized before epic verification\n\n- **TC-7.1a: Cleanup list compiled**\n  - Given: All story-level work is complete\n  - When: The run transitions toward epic verification\n  - Then: Deferred and accepted-risk items from prior story cycles are compiled into a durable cleanup artifact\n\n**AC-7.2:** Cleanup is reviewed before dispatch\n\n- **TC-7.2a: Human review of cleanup batch**\n  - Given: A cleanup artifact has been compiled\n  - When: The orchestrator prepares to dispatch cleanup work\n  - Then: The orchestrator reviews the categorized cleanup batch with the human before dispatching it\n\n**AC-7.3:** Epic verification starts from the cleaned state rather than skipping outstanding tracked items\n\n- **TC-7.3a: Cleanup before epic verification**\n  - Given: The cleanup phase has produced approved fixes\n  - When: The run proceeds to epic verification\n  - Then: The orchestrator verifies the cleanup result before launching epic-level verification\n\n**AC-8.1:** Epic-level verification is mandatory for every run\n\n- **TC-8.1a: Multi-story epic**\n  - Given: All stories in a multi-story epic are accepted\n  - When: The implementation run reaches epic closeout\n  - Then: The skill requires epic-level verification before final closeout\n- **TC-8.1b: No skip path**\n  - Given: The implementation run reaches epic closeout\n  - When: The orchestrator is deciding next steps\n  - Then: The skill does not treat epic-level verification as optional\n- **TC-8.1c: Epic-level mock and shim audit**\n  - Given: Epic-level verification is running\n  - When: Epic verifiers review the implemented system\n  - Then: They check for remaining inappropriate mocks, shims, placeholders, or fake adapters on production paths and report them explicitly\n\n**AC-8.2:** Epic synthesis is mandatory for every run\n\n- **TC-8.2a: Mandatory synthesis**\n  - Given: Epic verifier reports have been collected\n  - When: The epic verification workflow proceeds\n  - Then: The orchestrator launches the synthesis workflow every run\n\n**AC-8.3:** Epic synthesis is more than report merging\n\n- **TC-8.3a: Synthesizer verifies reported issues**\n  - Given: Epic verifier reports contain findings\n  - When: The synthesis workflow runs\n  - Then: The synthesizer independently checks and categorizes the reported issues before returning its result\n\n**AC-8.4:** The orchestrator runs final epic verification commands itself\n\n- **TC-8.4a: Final epic gate owned by orchestrator**\n  - Given: Epic verification and synthesis are complete\n  - When: The run is ready for final closeout\n  - Then: The orchestrator runs the final epic acceptance gate itself rather than relying only on verifier and synthesizer reports\n\n### Technical Design\n<!-- Jira: Technical Notes or sub-section of Description -->\n\n**Relevant Data Contracts**\n\n#### CLI Operations\n\n| Operation | Purpose | Required Inputs | Expected Outputs | Outcome States |\n|-----------|---------|-----------------|------------------|----------------|\n| Launch pre-epic cleanup | Resolve deferred and accepted-risk items before epic verification | cleanup batch artifact, selected fix workflow, working directory | cleanup result and updated verification state | cleaned, needs-more-cleanup, blocked |\n| Launch epic verifiers | Run epic-level verification | epic folder context, verifier configs, working directory | array of structured epic verifier results | pass, revise, block |\n| Launch epic synthesis | Synthesize epic verification results | epic verifier reports, synthesis config, working directory | synthesized epic result | ready-for-closeout, needs-fixes, needs-more-verification, blocked |\n\n#### Epic Cleanup Result\n\n| Field | Description |\n|-------|-------------|\n| cleanup batch path | The reviewed cleanup artifact used for the cleanup run |\n| files changed | Files changed during cleanup execution |\n| change summary | Short description of cleanup corrections applied |\n| gates run | Checks executed for the cleanup run |\n| unresolved concerns | Cleanup items or risks that remain open |\n| recommended next step | Whether the orchestrator should proceed, continue cleanup, or pause |\n| outcome | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, or `blocked` |\n\n#### Epic Verifier Result\n\n| Field | Description |\n|-------|-------------|\n| provider id | Provider/runtime used for the epic verifier |\n| reviewer label | Distinguishes reviewers in the epic batch |\n| cross-story findings | Integration and consistency findings |\n| architecture findings | End-to-end architecture issues |\n| epic AC/TC coverage assessment | Compliance and completeness findings |\n| mock or shim audit findings | Remaining inappropriate mocks, shims, placeholders, or fake adapters on production paths |\n| non-blocking and blocking findings | Categorized issues |\n| unresolved items | Evidence gaps or uncertain claims |\n| gate result | Result of the epic verification checks run by that verifier |\n\n#### Epic Synthesis Result\n\n| Field | Description |\n|-------|-------------|\n| confirmed issues | Findings the synthesizer validated |\n| disputed or unconfirmed issues | Findings not validated cleanly |\n| readiness assessment | Whether the epic appears ready for closeout |\n| recommended next step | Additional fixes, additional verification, or closeout discussion |\n\n#### Implementation Log Requirements Relevant to Closeout\n\nThe log must record:\n\n- pre-epic-cleanup status\n- epic-verification and synthesis status\n- artifact paths used by the run\n- enough state for the orchestrator to resume after interruption without reconstructing the run from conversation context\n\n**Relevant Tech Design Sections**\n\n- `tech-design-skill-process.md` -> Flow 3: Orchestrator State Ownership, Flow 4: Process Playbook and Command Routing\n- `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` -> Flow 4: Epic Cleanup, Verification, and Synthesis, Epic Cleanup Result, Epic Verifier Result, Epic Synthesis Result, Artifact Persistence Layout\n- `test-plan.md` -> cleanup, epic verifier, synthesis, CLI guide, and CLI IO contract suites\n\n*See the tech design document for full architecture, implementation targets, and test mapping.*\n\n### Definition of Done\n<!-- Jira: Definition of Done or Acceptance Criteria footer -->\n\n- Deferred and accepted-risk items are materialized before epic verification\n- Cleanup review remains orchestrator/human owned before dispatch\n- Epic verifier and synthesizer workflows both run before closeout\n- Final epic gate remains orchestrator-owned\n- Cleanup, epic verification, and synthesis artifacts are persisted for durable recovery and review\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-08/006-verifier-codex53-high.jsonl:7:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_2","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/tech-design.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Technical Design: ls-claude-impl\n\n## Purpose\n\nThis design translates the `ls-claude-impl` epic into an implementable architecture for a new Claude Code orchestration skill plus a Node-compatible implementation CLI. The feature has two dense domains that need different levels of treatment: the orchestrator-facing skill surface and the CLI/runtime surface that executes bounded operations for multiple secondary harnesses. For that reason this design uses **Config B**:\n\n| Document | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `tech-design.md` | Decision record, context, system view, top-tier surfaces, module architecture overview, dependency and packaging decisions, work breakdown |\n| `tech-design-skill-process.md` | The orchestrator-facing skill surface: progressive disclosure docs, reading journey, state ownership, process playbook, stage transitions |\n| `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` | The CLI/runtime surface: command model, config schemas, prompt asset system, provider adapters, operation contracts, packaging |\n| `test-plan.md` | TC-to-test mapping, mock strategy, fixture strategy, test counts, chunk-level TDD plan |\n\nThe index stays small enough to be navigable while still recording the cross-cutting decisions that both companion docs inherit. The companion split follows the real boundary in this feature: one side teaches the orchestrator how the process works, the other side executes the process through a clean agent-first CLI.\n\n## Spec Validation\n\nThe epic is designable. Its major flows, ACs, and TCs describe a coherent system with clear public seams: spec-pack discovery, run configuration, prompt assembly, retained implementation sessions, fresh verification, cleanup, and epic closeout. The epic is unusually detailed for a skill feature, but the added specificity is appropriate because the feature is not just a prose skill; it is a skill plus a runtime helper plus a prompt system plus a result-contract surface.\n\nThe design does make three clarifications that should be recorded explicitly before implementation begins. None of them block design, but each one turns an implied behavior into a concrete contract the implementation can build around.\n\n| Issue | Spec Location | Resolution | Status |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| The epic requires the orchestrator to record role defaults and run choices, but it does not name a dedicated machine-readable run config file. | AC-2.4, Data Contracts, `team-impl-log.md` requirements | Introduce `impl-run.config.json` at the spec-pack root as the orchestrator-owned run configuration. `team-impl-log.md` remains the durable narrative/state record. | Resolved — clarified |\n| The epic distinguishes Codex/Copilot-backed GPT lanes from Claude-only fallback, but the harness terminology needs to preserve Claude Code as the always-present primary harness. | AC-2.2, AC-2.3, Role Model Matrix | Use `primary_harness: \"claude-code\"` plus per-role `secondary_harness: \"codex\" | \"copilot\" | \"none\"`. `none` means the role uses the primary harness only. | Resolved — clarified |\n| The epic says the CLI should be stateless across calls, but story implementation also requires retained implementor continuity. | Constraint C3, AC-4.2, AC-6.3 | Keep orchestration state outside the CLI. The CLI may emit continuation/session handles for provider sessions, but the orchestrator owns persistence, routing, and recovery through `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any future orchestrator-managed support files. | Resolved — clarified |\n| The epic allows Copilot as the generic GPT fallback, but v1 Copilot support is limited to fresh-session roles while story implementation requires retained continuation. | AC-2.2, AC-4.2, Role Model Matrix | In v1, disallow Copilot for `story_implementor`. Retained implementor fallback order is Codex -> Claude Code primary harness. Copilot remains valid for fresh verifier, quick-fix, and synthesis roles. | Resolved — deviated |\n| The epic requires the packaged skill to include the CLI, but the current build surface only composes markdown skill outputs and bundled references. | Feature overview, CLI responsibilities implied by scope | Add a build step that bundles the modular CLI source into a single Node-compatible JS file and copies that file into the packaged skill output. | Resolved — deviated |\n\n### Tech Design Question Coverage\n\nThe epic’s Tech Design Questions are answered by this pack as follows:\n\n| Question | Primary Answer Location |\n|---|---|\n| Public CLI commands, arguments, exit codes, result formats | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Command Surface, Result Contracts |\n| Prompt assets storage and assembly | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Prompt Asset System |\n| Three self-review passes | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |\n| Model matrix and harness fallback | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Run Config + Provider Adapters |\n| Continuation identifiers | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Session Continuation Contracts |\n| Exact result fields and serialization | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Result Contracts |\n| `team-impl-log.md` state model | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Orchestrator State and Durable Artifacts |\n| How orchestrator consumes CLI results | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Process Playbook and Stage Transitions |\n| Verification-gate hardening | `tech-design.md` — Verification Scripts; `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Initialization Flow |\n| Companion tech-design naming convention | This index — document structure and companion naming |\n| Implementor replacement vs continuation | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Continue Rules |\n| Quick-fix narrow contract | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Quick Fix Workflow |\n| Pre-epic cleanup artifacts | `tech-design-skill-process.md` and `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Epic Cleanup |\n| Self-review prompt evolution and stop rules | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |\n\n## Context\n\n`liminal-spec` is primarily a content-and-build repository, not a general application runtime. Most skills in the repo are markdown-first artifacts composed from `src/` and packaged into installable skill folders or standalone markdown files. That matters because `ls-claude-impl` is not a normal addition to the pack. It introduces a second kind of deliverable inside the same repo: a bundled runtime helper that must ship inside the packaged skill while still fitting the repository’s content-composition workflow.\n\nThe problem being solved is reliability of implementation orchestration, not lack of methodology. Existing implementation skill surfaces carry useful process ideas, but they rely on too much live runtime improvisation and too many layers between the orchestrator and the actual work. The new design narrows the system to three stable public surfaces. First, the skill teaches the orchestrator the process and the reading journey. Second, the orchestrator owns state and decision-making. Third, the CLI executes bounded operations and returns disciplined machine-readable results. The center of gravity shifts away from “agent teams all the way down” and toward “one orchestrator with explicit process docs and a legible execution surface.”\n\nThe future direction also matters now. The CLI is not just a local helper for this skill. It is an early public execution surface that is expected to map cleanly into a later SDK and then into web-app harness tools. That creates strong pressure toward explicit inputs, outputs, and outcome states. Hidden orchestration state inside the CLI would make later extraction harder. For the same reason, the skill should own the explanation of the process while the CLI owns execution. The orchestrator needs to understand what is happening at the process level without carrying implementation detail that belongs inside the runtime.\n\nA second pressure comes from prompt architecture. This feature does not only need CLI commands; it needs stable role prompts, reusable snippets, public insert hooks, and role-fit reading journeys. Those prompt assets are implementation-critical. They are not just docs, and they are not just helper strings. They are part of the runtime contract. The design therefore treats the prompt system as a first-class architectural surface rather than as glue hidden inside an implementation file.\n\nFinally, the repo’s existing build pipeline constrains how the feature lands. The current build emits markdown skill directories plus standalone markdown packs. It does not yet package executable runtime files into skill outputs. The design therefore includes build-system changes as part of the feature instead of leaving them to Story 0 discovery. The packaged skill must contain everything the orchestrator needs at runtime: markdown guidance plus a single bundled Node-compatible CLI file.\n\n## System View\n\nThere is no separate technical architecture document for this work. The top-tier surfaces below are therefore locally derived from the epic, nearby design notes, the existing `codex-impl` runtime, and the current repository layout.\n\n### Top-Tier Surfaces\n\n| Surface | Source | This Epic's Role |\n|---|---|---|\n| Skill Surface | Locally derived | `ls-claude-impl` markdown entrypoint and progressive-disclosure references that onboard the orchestrator and teach the process |\n| Runtime Surface | Locally derived | `ls-impl-cli` command surface, result contracts, provider adapters, prompt assembly, and packaging |\n| Build Surface | Locally derived | Manifest/build integration that composes the skill and embeds the bundled CLI artifact into packaged outputs |\n\n### System Context Diagram\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n    User[Human user] --> CC[Claude Code orchestrator]\n    CC --> Skill[ls-claude-impl SKILL.md]\n    Skill --> RefA[reading-journey.md]\n    Skill --> RefB[process-playbook.md]\n    Skill --> RefC[cli-operations.md]\n    Skill --> RefD[prompt-system.md]\n\n    CC --> SpecPack[Spec pack root]\n    SpecPack --> Epic[epic.md]\n    SpecPack --> TD[tech-design*.md]\n    SpecPack --> TP[test-plan.md]\n    SpecPack --> Stories[stories/]\n    SpecPack --> RunConfig[impl-run.config.json]\n    SpecPack --> Log[team-impl-log.md]\n\n    CC --> CLI[ls-impl-cli]\n    CLI --> PromptAssets[Embedded base prompts + snippets]\n    CLI --> Codex[Codex CLI]\n    CLI --> Copilot[Copilot CLI]\n    CLI --> Claude[Claude Code CLI]\n\n    CLI --> Results[Structured JSON results]\n    Results --> CC\n    CC --> Log\n```\n\n### External Contracts\n\nThe external contracts are narrower than the whole feature. They are the fixed points the orchestrator and CLI must agree on before implementation begins.\n\n#### Incoming Contracts\n\n| Input | Required | Source | Purpose |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `epic.md` | yes | spec-pack root | Functional source of truth |\n| `tech-design.md` | yes | spec-pack root | Design index consumed by the orchestrator and some CLI operations |\n| `test-plan.md` | yes | spec-pack root | TC→test mapping and verification strategy |\n| `tech-design-*.md` companions | conditional | spec-pack root | Required only when the tech-design config is 4-doc |\n| `stories/` | yes | spec-pack root | Ordered story slices for implementation |\n| `impl-run.config.json` | yes | orchestrator-authored | Role/harness/model configuration for runtime operations |\n| `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` | no | spec-pack root | Optional public insert for implementor prompt assembly |\n| `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` | no | spec-pack root | Optional public insert for verifier prompt assembly |\n\n#### Outgoing Contracts\n\n| Output | Producer | Destination | Purpose |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `team-impl-log.md` updates | orchestrator | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration record and recovery state |\n| Structured command result JSON | CLI | stdout / orchestrator ingest | Machine-readable completion surface for each operation |\n| Prompt-ready execution payloads | CLI | secondary harnesses | Stable provider-facing prompt contract |\n| Bundled CLI artifact | build surface | packaged skill output | Runtime executable shipped with the skill |\n\n#### Outcome States\n\nThe CLI should use explicit outcome states instead of ambiguous free-text status messages.\n\n| Operation Class | Allowed Outcome States |\n|---|---|\n| `inspect`, `preflight` | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` |\n| implementation / quick-fix operations | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` |\n| verifier batches | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |\n| cleanup operations | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` |\n| synthesis operations | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |\n\n#### Runtime Prerequisites\n\n| Prerequisite | Where Needed | How to Verify |\n|---|---|---|\n| Bun 1.3.x+ | local development, build, tests | `bun --version` |\n| Node-compatible runtime | packaged CLI execution | `node -v` |\n| Git repository root | CLI commands that inspect repo/worktree context | `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` |\n| `codex` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: \"codex\"` | `codex --version` plus auth/smoke command |\n| `copilot` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: \"copilot\"` | `copilot --version` plus auth/smoke command |\n| `claude` CLI | always, because primary harness is Claude Code | `claude --version` plus auth/smoke command |\n\n## Module Architecture\n\nThis feature needs changes in three repository areas: skill content, runtime process code, and build/packaging glue. The layout below keeps those responsibilities separated while making the packaged skill self-contained.\n\n```text\nsrc/\n├── phases/\n│   └── claude-impl.md                         # NEW: orchestration skill entrypoint\n├── references/\n│   ├── claude-impl-reading-journey.md         # NEW: post-setup reading journey\n│   ├── claude-impl-process-playbook.md        # NEW: full stage-by-stage process guide\n│   ├── claude-impl-cli-operations.md          # NEW: public CLI usage reference\n│   └── claude-impl-prompt-system.md           # NEW: prompt-system guide for orchestrator understanding\n└── README-pack.md / README-markdown-pack.md   # MODIFIED: new skill listed in pack docs\n\nprocesses/\n└── impl-cli/\n    ├── cli.ts                                 # NEW: citty entrypoint\n    ├── commands/\n    │   ├── inspect.ts\n    │   ├── preflight.ts\n    │   ├── story-implement.ts\n    │   ├── story-continue.ts\n    │   ├── story-verify.ts\n    │   ├── quick-fix.ts\n    │   ├── epic-cleanup.ts\n    │   ├── epic-verify.ts\n    │   └── epic-synthesize.ts\n    ├── core/\n    │   ├── config-schema.ts\n    │   ├── spec-pack.ts\n    │   ├── story-order.ts\n    │   ├── prompt-assets.ts\n    │   ├── prompt-assembly.ts\n    │   ├── provider-checks.ts\n    │   ├── provider-adapters/\n    │   │   ├── claude-code.ts\n    │   │   ├── codex.ts\n    │   │   └── copilot.ts\n    │   ├── result-contracts.ts\n    │   ├── status-codes.ts\n    │   └── embedded-assets.generated.ts\n    ├── prompts/\n    │   ├── base/\n    │   └── snippets/\n    └── tests/\n\nscripts/\n├── sync-impl-cli-assets.ts                    # NEW: embed prompt assets into generated TS\n├── build.ts                                   # MODIFIED: bundle/copy CLI artifact into packaged skill\n└── validate.ts                                # MODIFIED: validate packaged CLI artifact presence\n\nmanifest.json                                  # MODIFIED: add ls-claude-impl skill and bundled artifact metadata\n```\n\n### Module Responsibility Matrix\n\n| Module | Status | Responsibility | Dependencies | ACs Covered |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `src/phases/claude-impl.md` | NEW | Initial orchestrator onboarding, setup flow, state ownership rules, progressive disclosure entrypoint | references, packaged CLI path contract | AC-1.3 to AC-1.6, AC-2.4, AC-6.1 to AC-6.3 |\n| `processes/impl-cli/cli.ts` | NEW | Top-level citty command entrypoint and subcommand registration | command modules | AC-4.1, AC-5.1, AC-8.1 to AC-8.4 |\n| `processes/impl-cli/core/config-schema.ts` | NEW | Parse and validate `impl-run.config.json` | `c12`, `zod` | AC-2.2 to AC-2.4 |\n| `processes/impl-cli/core/spec-pack.ts` | NEW | Resolve spec-pack files, story inventory, insert files, tech-design shape | filesystem | AC-1.1 to AC-1.6 |\n| `processes/impl-cli/core/prompt-assets.ts` + `prompt-assembly.ts` | NEW | Load embedded prompt assets and assemble role prompts deterministically | generated asset module, public inserts | AC-3.1 to AC-3.4, AC-4.3 |\n| `processes/impl-cli/core/provider-checks.ts` + `provider-adapters/*` | NEW | Detect harness availability, invoke provider CLIs, resume sessions, normalize outputs | child process, provider CLIs | AC-2.1 to AC-2.4, AC-4.2, AC-5.1, AC-8.1 |\n| `processes/impl-cli/core/result-contracts.ts` | NEW | Shared result schemas and markdown/report contracts for all operations | `zod` | AC-4.5, AC-5.2, AC-7.1, AC-8.2 to AC-8.4 |\n| `scripts/sync-impl-cli-assets.ts` | NEW | Generate embedded prompt-asset module from markdown prompt sources | prompt files | AC-3.1 to AC-3.4 |\n| `scripts/build.ts` and `manifest.json` | MODIFIED | Bundle the CLI into a single Node-compatible JS file and include it in packaged skill output | Bun bundler, manifest metadata | AC-1.1, AC-3.1, packaging support for full feature |\n\n### Component Interaction Diagram\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart TD\n    O[Claude Code orchestrator]\n    S[ls-claude-impl skill files]\n    C[ls-impl-cli command]\n    SP[spec-pack resolver]\n    CFG[run config schema]\n    PA[prompt assembly]\n    AD[provider adapter]\n    PR[provider CLI runtime]\n    R[result contract]\n\n    O --> S\n    O --> C\n    C --> SP\n    C --> CFG\n    C --> PA\n    PA --> AD\n    AD --> PR\n    PR --> R\n    R --> O\n```\n\nThe key boundary is that the orchestrator uses the skill to understand what should happen, then uses the CLI to make one bounded thing happen, then writes the result into its own durable state. The CLI does not own orchestration lifecycle between calls.\n\n### Manifest and Build Metadata\n\nThe build surface needs one new manifest-level concept: a bundled runtime artifact that belongs inside the packaged skill even though it does not belong inside the composed markdown body.\n\nProposed shape:\n\n```json\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-08/006-verifier-codex53-high.jsonl:9:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_3","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/tech-design-skill-process.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Technical Design Companion: Skill and Process Surface\n\n## Purpose\n\nThis companion covers the orchestrator-facing side of `ls-claude-impl`: the markdown skill entrypoint, the progressive-disclosure references, the orchestrator-owned state artifacts, and the process playbook that tells Claude Code what to do and when to use the CLI. The goal of this surface is not to execute work directly. Its job is to minimize cognitive load for the orchestrator while still giving the orchestrator enough understanding to supervise the implementation process critically.\n\nThe orchestrator should never have to reverse-engineer the process from CLI help text or scattered repository notes. The skill surface should answer five questions cleanly:\n\n1. What artifacts must exist before a run can begin?\n2. What should the orchestrator read first, and what can wait?\n3. What state does the orchestrator own and update?\n4. Which CLI operation should be used next?\n5. What evidence is required before the orchestrator can progress?\n\n## Skill Surface Layout\n\nThe skill should not be a monolith. It should load in layers, with each deeper file answering the question raised by the previous layer.\n\n| File | Role in the Workflow | Why It Exists |\n|---|---|---|\n| `src/phases/claude-impl.md` | Startup orientation and setup contract | Keeps initial context short and action-oriented |\n| `src/references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md` | Post-setup reading order for the spec pack | Avoids loading the full artifact world before the orchestrator knows where to look |\n| `src/references/claude-impl-process-playbook.md` | Stage-by-stage process rules | Gives the orchestrator one durable place to learn transitions, receipts, and escalation behavior |\n| `src/references/claude-impl-cli-operations.md` | Public operation guide | Keeps command semantics explicit without bloating `SKILL.md` |\n| `src/references/claude-impl-prompt-system.md` | Prompt architecture and insert explanation | Explains the runtime prompt model at the process level without exposing provider-specific implementation details inline |\n\nThe orchestrator starts with `SKILL.md`, resolves the spec-pack location, performs the minimum initial checks, and only then reads the reading-journey file. That file tells the orchestrator what to read from the spec pack and what to extract into durable state before deeper runtime work begins.\n\n### Skill-Pack Layout in Dist Output\n\nThe packaged skill should land with this shape:\n\n```text\ndist/skills/ls-claude-impl/\n├── SKILL.md\n├── references/\n│   ├── claude-impl-reading-journey.md\n│   ├── claude-impl-process-playbook.md\n│   ├── claude-impl-cli-operations.md\n│   └── claude-impl-prompt-system.md\n└── bin/\n    └── ls-impl-cli.cjs\n```\n\nThe runtime file lives beside the docs, but the docs remain the authoritative explanation of the process. The orchestrator should be able to succeed by reading the docs and then using the CLI. The CLI should not have to teach the methodology.\n\n## Orchestrator-Owned Artifacts\n\nThe orchestrator is the state authority for a run. The CLI is not the owner of run lifecycle, progression, or recovery. That boundary is what keeps the future SDK/harness surface clean.\n\n### `impl-run.config.json`\n\n`impl-run.config.json` is the machine-readable declaration of the intended run shape. The orchestrator authors it or updates it during initialization. The CLI validates it before any provider-backed work begins.\n\n```json\n{\n  \"version\": 1,\n  \"primary_harness\": \"claude-code\",\n  \"story_implementor\": {\n    \"secondary_harness\": \"codex\",\n    \"model\": \"gpt-5.4\",\n    \"reasoning_effort\": \"high\"\n  },\n  \"quick_fixer\": {\n    \"secondary_harness\": \"codex\",\n    \"model\": \"gpt-5.4\",\n    \"reasoning_effort\": \"medium\"\n  },\n  \"story_verifier_1\": {\n    \"secondary_harness\": \"codex\",\n    \"model\": \"gpt-5.4\",\n    \"reasoning_effort\": \"xhigh\"\n  },\n  \"story_verifier_2\": {\n    \"secondary_harness\": \"none\",\n    \"model\": \"claude-sonnet\",\n    \"reasoning_effort\": \"high\"\n  },\n  \"self_review\": {\n    \"passes\": 3\n  },\n  \"epic_verifiers\": [\n    {\n      \"label\": \"epic-verifier-1\",\n      \"secondary_harness\": \"codex\",\n      \"model\": \"gpt-5.4\",\n      \"reasoning_effort\": \"xhigh\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"label\": \"epic-verifier-2\",\n      \"secondary_harness\": \"none\",\n      \"model\": \"claude-sonnet\",\n      \"reasoning_effort\": \"high\"\n    }\n  ],\n  \"epic_synthesizer\": {\n    \"secondary_harness\": \"codex\",\n    \"model\": \"gpt-5.4\",\n    \"reasoning_effort\": \"xhigh\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThe key simplicity rule is that the file should only say what the orchestrator actually needs to decide: the primary harness, the per-role secondary harness, the model selection, and the self-review pass count. It should not carry derived values, convenience aliases, or runtime details that the orchestrator does not need to reason about.\n\n### Default Resolution Algorithm\n\nThe orchestrator owns the config file, but it should not invent defaults ad hoc. The skill surface should teach one deterministic default-generation algorithm:\n\n1. Set `primary_harness` to `claude-code`.\n2. Probe secondary harness availability in this order:\n   - Codex available\n   - else Copilot available\n   - else no secondary harness\n3. If Codex is available, use Codex as the default secondary harness for all GPT-lane roles:\n   - story implementor: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `high`\n   - quick fixer: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`\n   - story verifier 1: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   - epic verifier 1: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   - epic synthesizer: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n4. If Codex is unavailable but Copilot is available, use Copilot only for fresh-session roles:\n   - story implementor: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   - quick fixer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`\n   - story verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   - epic verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\n   - epic synthesizer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\n   Story implementor still falls back to `secondary_harness: \"none\"` because v1 retained implementor continuation is not defined for Copilot.\n5. If no GPT secondary harness is available, switch all roles to `secondary_harness: \"none\"` and record a degraded-diversity condition in `team-impl-log.md`.\n\nThis keeps config authorship with the orchestrator while still satisfying the epic’s requirement that defaults and fallback behavior be resolved deterministically before implementation begins.\n\n### `team-impl-log.md`\n\n`team-impl-log.md` is the durable orchestration record. It is the primary run-state artifact, not a narrative afterthought. The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.\n\nRequired sections:\n\n| Section | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| Run Overview | spec-pack root, current status, current story, current phase |\n| Run Configuration | snapshot of the validated `impl-run.config.json` choices |\n| Verification Gates | story and epic gate commands discovered from project policy |\n| Story Sequence | full story list and current position |\n| Receipts | pre-acceptance evidence and dispositions by story |\n| Cumulative Baselines | test-count expectations and regression checks |\n| Cleanup / Epic Verification | deferred items, cleanup status, final verification state |\n| Open Risks / Accepted Risks | explicit unresolved or accepted-risk items |\n\nThe log remains markdown because it is both human-facing and agent-facing. The orchestrator needs to read and update it directly.     27	The orchestrator starts with `SKILL.md`, resolves the spec-pack location, performs the minimum initial checks, and only then reads the reading-journey file. That file tells the orchestrator what to read from the spec pack and what to extract into durable state before deeper runtime work begins.
    28	
    29	### Skill-Pack Layout in Dist Output
    30	
    31	The packaged skill should land with this shape:
    32	
    33	```text
    34	dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/
    35	├── SKILL.md
    36	├── references/
    37	│   ├── claude-impl-reading-journey.md
    38	│   ├── claude-impl-process-playbook.md
    39	│   ├── claude-impl-cli-operations.md
    40	│   └── claude-impl-prompt-system.md
    41	└── bin/
    42	    └── ls-impl-cli.cjs
    43	```
    44	
    45	The runtime file lives beside the docs, but the docs remain the authoritative explanation of the process. The orchestrator should be able to succeed by reading the docs and then using the CLI. The CLI should not have to teach the methodology.
    46	
    47	## Orchestrator-Owned Artifacts
    48	
    49	The orchestrator is the state authority for a run. The CLI is not the owner of run lifecycle, progression, or recovery. That boundary is what keeps the future SDK/harness surface clean.
    50	
    51	### `impl-run.config.json`
    52	
    53	`impl-run.config.json` is the machine-readable declaration of the intended run shape. The orchestrator authors it or updates it during initialization. The CLI validates it before any provider-backed work begins.
    54	
    55	```json
    56	{
    57	  "version": 1,
    58	  "primary_harness": "claude-code",
    59	  "story_implementor": {
    60	    "secondary_harness": "codex",
    61	    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    62	    "reasoning_effort": "high"
    63	  },
    64	  "quick_fixer": {
    65	    "secondary_harness": "codex",
    66	    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    67	    "reasoning_effort": "medium"
    68	  },
    69	  "story_verifier_1": {
    70	    "secondary_harness": "codex",
    71	    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    72	    "reasoning_effort": "xhigh"
    73	  },
    74	  "story_verifier_2": {
    75	    "secondary_harness": "none",
    76	    "model": "claude-sonnet",
    77	    "reasoning_effort": "high"
    78	  },
    79	  "self_review": {
    80	    "passes": 3
    81	  },
    82	  "epic_verifiers": [
    83	    {
    84	      "label": "epic-verifier-1",
    85	      "secondary_harness": "codex",
    86	      "model": "gpt-5.4",
    87	      "reasoning_effort": "xhigh"
    88	    },
    89	    {
    90	      "label": "epic-verifier-2",
    91	      "secondary_harness": "none",
    92	      "model": "claude-sonnet",
    93	      "reasoning_effort": "high"
    94	    }
    95	  ],
    96	  "epic_synthesizer": {
    97	    "secondary_harness": "codex",
    98	    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    99	    "reasoning_effort": "xhigh"
   100	  }
   101	}
   102	```
   103	
   104	The key simplicity rule is that the file should only say what the orchestrator actually needs to decide: the primary harness, the per-role secondary harness, the model selection, and the self-review pass count. It should not carry derived values, convenience aliases, or runtime details that the orchestrator does not need to reason about.
   105	
   106	### Default Resolution Algorithm
   107	
   108	The orchestrator owns the config file, but it should not invent defaults ad hoc. The skill surface should teach one deterministic default-generation algorithm:
   109	
   110	1. Set `primary_harness` to `claude-code`.
   111	2. Probe secondary harness availability in this order:
   112	   - Codex available
   113	   - else Copilot available
   114	   - else no secondary harness
   115	3. If Codex is available, use Codex as the default secondary harness for all GPT-lane roles:
   116	   - story implementor: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `high`
   117	   - quick fixer: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`
   118	   - story verifier 1: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   119	   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`
   120	   - epic verifier 1: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   121	   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`
   122	   - epic synthesizer: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   123	4. If Codex is unavailable but Copilot is available, use Copilot only for fresh-session roles:
   124	   - story implementor: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`
   125	   - quick fixer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`
   126	   - story verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   127	   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`
   128	   - epic verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   129	   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`
   130	   - epic synthesizer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   131	   Story implementor still falls back to `secondary_harness: "none"` because v1 retained implementor continuation is not defined for Copilot.
   132	5. If no GPT secondary harness is available, switch all roles to `secondary_harness: "none"` and record a degraded-diversity condition in `team-impl-log.md`.
   133	
   134	This keeps config authorship with the orchestrator while still satisfying the epic’s requirement that defaults and fallback behavior be resolved deterministically before implementation begins.
   135	
   136	### `team-impl-log.md`
   137	
   138	`team-impl-log.md` is the durable orchestration record. It is the primary run-state artifact, not a narrative afterthought. The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.
   139	
   140	Required sections:
   141	
   142	| Section | Purpose |
   143	|---|---|
   144	| Run Overview | spec-pack root, current status, current story, current phase |
   145	| Run Configuration | snapshot of the validated `impl-run.config.json` choices |
   146	| Verification Gates | story and epic gate commands discovered from project policy |
   147	| Story Sequence | full story list and current position |
   148	| Receipts | pre-acceptance evidence and dispositions by story |
   149	| Cumulative Baselines | test-count expectations and regression checks |
   150	| Cleanup / Epic Verification | deferred items, cleanup status, final verification state |
   151	| Open Risks / Accepted Risks | explicit unresolved or accepted-risk items |
   152	
   153	The log remains markdown because it is both human-facing and agent-facing. The orchestrator needs to read and update it directly. A second support file can be added later if highly structured state becomes necessary, but the v1 feature should keep the required surface small.
   154	
   155	### State Enum
   156	
   157	The log should carry one explicit current-state field using this enum:
   158	
   159	| State | Meaning |
   160	|---|---|
   161	| `SETUP` | skill loaded, spec-pack root being resolved, config/log initialization not complete |
   162	| `BETWEEN_STORIES` | ready to start the next story |
   163	| `STORY_ACTIVE` | implementation / verification / fix routing in progress for one story |
   164	| `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY` | all stories accepted, cleanup and epic closeout not yet complete |
   165	| `EPIC_VERIFY_ACTIVE` | cleanup, epic verifier batch, or synthesis is in progress |
   166	| `COMPLETE` | run complete |
   167	| `FAILED` | blocked or failed terminally pending human decision |
   168

   234	The orchestrator can add prose beneath these headings, but these sections and labels should be present so recovery is deterministic.

   237

    94	| `story-verify` | Run a fresh verifier batch for one story | story id, run config, verification context | verifier batch result array | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |
    95	| `quick-fix` | Apply a narrow, bounded fix request | fix request, affected scope, role config | quick-fix result | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-more-routing`, `blocked` |
    96	| `epic-cleanup` | Apply cleanup-only corrections before epic verification | cleanup batch, selected fix workflow | cleanup result | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` |
    97	| `epic-verify` | Run fresh epic-level verification | spec-pack root, run config | epic verifier result array | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |
    98	| `epic-synthesize` | Verify and consolidate epic-level findings | epic verifier results, run config | synthesis result | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |
    99	
   100	The CLI with no arguments should print quick-start help only. The full process explanation stays in the skill docs.
   101	
   102	### Command Signatures
   103	
   104	The public signatures should stay stable even if internal implementation changes.
   105	
   106	| Command | Signature |
   107	|---|---|
   108	| `inspect` | `ls-impl-cli inspect --spec-pack-root <path> [--json]` |
   109	| `preflight` | `ls-impl-cli preflight --spec-pack-root <path> [--config <path>] [--story-gate <cmd>] [--epic-gate <cmd>] [--json]` |
   110	| `story-implement` | `ls-impl-cli story-implement --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   111	| `story-continue` | `ls-impl-cli story-continue --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> --provider <provider> --session-id <id> [--config <path>] [--followup-file <path> | --followup-text <text>] [--json]` |
   112	| `story-verify` | `ls-impl-cli story-verify --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   113	| `quick-fix` | `ls-impl-cli quick-fix --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> --request-file <path> [--scope-file <path>] [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   114	| `epic-cleanup` | `ls-impl-cli epic-cleanup --spec-pack-root <path> --cleanup-batch <path> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   115	| `epic-verify` | `ls-impl-cli epic-verify --spec-pack-root <path> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   116	| `epic-synthesize` | `ls-impl-cli epic-synthesize --spec-pack-root <path> --verifier-report <path> --verifier-report <path> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   117	
   118	All commands should support `--json` for machine-readable output. Human-readable summaries may exist for manual use, but the orchestrator contract should be the JSON payload.
   119	
   120	### Public CLI IO Contract
   121	
   122	The CLI should behave consistently across commands.
   123	
   124	| Surface | Rule |
   125	|---|---|
   126	| stdout with `--json` | emit exactly one `CliResultEnvelope<T>` JSON object |
   127	| stdout without `--json` | emit a concise human-readable summary only |
   128	| stderr | reserved for progress/debugging; never required for orchestration routing |
   129	| persisted artifacts | write the same JSON envelope that was emitted on stdout |
   130	| default config path | `<spec-pack-root>/impl-run.config.json` unless `--config` overrides it |
   131	| default artifact root | `<spec-pack-root>/artifacts/` |
   132	
   133	### Command Examples
   134	
   135	```bash
   136	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs inspect --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --json
   137	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs preflight --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --json
   138	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs story-implement --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --story-id story-03 --json
   139	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs story-verify --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --story-id story-03 --json
   140	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs epic-cleanup --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --cleanup-batch /abs/spec-pack/artifacts/cleanup/cleanup-batch.md --json
   141	```
   142	
   143	For commands whose inner payload also contains an `outcome`, the envelope `outcome` is the routing source of truth and must match the inner payload exactly. Schema validation should reject mismatches.
   144	
   145	### Exit Codes
   146	
   147	Exit codes are a coarse routing signal. The JSON payload remains the source of truth.
   148	
   149	| Exit Code | Meaning |
   150	|---|---|
   151	| `0` | Command completed and produced a non-blocking outcome (`ready`, `ready-for-verification`, `pass`, `cleaned`, `ready-for-closeout`) |
   152	| `2` | Command completed but requires follow-up (`needs-user-decision`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `revise`, `needs-more-routing`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`) |
   153	| `3` | Command is blocked by environment, spec-pack, config, or provider readiness issue |
   154	| `1` | Invalid invocation, schema parse failure, or uncaught runtime error |
   155	
   156	### Result Routing Matrix
   157	
   158	This matrix is authoritative for handler implementation and tests.
   159	
   160	| Command Outcome | Envelope Status | Exit Code | Orchestrator Action |
   161	|---|---|---:|---|
   162	| `ready` | `ok` | 0 | proceed to next setup step |
   163	| `ready-for-verification` | `ok` | 0 | run verification or next bounded step |
   164	| `pass` | `ok` | 0 | evaluate for acceptance or next stage |
   165	| `cleaned` | `ok` | 0 | move into epic verification |
   166	| `ready-for-closeout` | `ok` | 0 | run final orchestrator-owned gate |
   167	| `needs-user-decision` | `needs-user-decision` | 2 | pause and request human clarification |
   168	| `needs-followup-fix` | `ok` | 2 | route same-session follow-up or fix path |
   169	| `needs-human-ruling` | `needs-user-decision` | 2 | pause for orchestrator/human ruling |
   170	| `revise` | `ok` | 2 | route fix and rerun verification |
   171	| `needs-more-routing` | `ok` | 2 | select another bounded correction path |
   172	| `needs-more-cleanup` | `ok` | 2 | continue cleanup cycle |
   173	| `needs-fixes` | `ok` | 2 | route fixes before closeout |
   174	| `needs-more-verification` | `ok` | 2 | launch additional verification/synthesis |
   175	| `blocked` | `blocked` | 3 | stop and inspect blocking error details |
   176	| parse/schema/runtime failure | `error` | 1 | treat as CLI failure rather than feature-state outcome |
   177	
   178	## Runtime Module Layout
   179	
   180	```text
   181	processes/impl-cli/
   182	├── cli.ts

   184	│   ├── inspect.ts
   185	│   ├── preflight.ts
   186	│   ├── story-implement.ts
   187	│   ├── story-continue.ts
   188	│   ├── story-verify.ts

   192	│   └── epic-synthesize.ts
   193	├── core/
   194	│   ├── config-schema.ts
   195	│   ├── spec-pack.ts
   196	│   ├── story-order.ts
   197	│   ├── prompt-assets.ts
   198	│   ├── prompt-assembly.ts
   199	│   ├── provider-checks.ts
   200	│   ├── provider-adapters/
   201	│   │   ├── claude-code.ts
   202	│   │   ├── codex.ts
   203	│   │   └── copilot.ts
   204	│   ├── result-contracts.ts

   211	```
   212	
   213	The command modules stay thin. They parse command-specific flags with `citty`, call into core services, and print structured JSON. All real behavior lives in `core/`.
   214	
   215	### Core Service Interfaces
   216	
   217	The command handlers should depend on a small set of explicit core interfaces.
   218	
   219	```typescript
   220	export interface SpecPackResolver {
   221	  resolve(specPackRoot: string): Promise<InspectResult>;
   222	}
   223	
   224	export interface VerificationGateResolver {
   225	  resolve(input: {
   226	    specPackRoot: string;
   227	    explicitStoryGate?: string;
   228	    explicitEpicGate?: string;
   229	  }): Promise<VerificationGateConfig | CliError>;
   230	}
   231	
   232	export interface ArtifactWriter {
   233	  write<T>(params: {
   234	    specPackRoot: string;
   235	    command: string;
   236	    storyId?: string;
   237	    payload: CliResultEnvelope<T>;
   238	  }): Promise<CliArtifactRef>;
   239	}
   240	
   241	export interface ProviderResultParser<T> {
   242	  parse(raw: ProviderExecutionResult): Promise<T | CliError>;
   243	}
   244	```
   245	
   246	These interfaces are deliberately smaller than the whole runtime. They pin the boundaries that are most likely to drift during implementation.
   247	
   248	## Run Configuration Contract
   249	
   250	The CLI consumes an explicit orchestrator-owned JSON file. The contract is intentionally lean.
   251	
   252	```typescript
   253	export type PrimaryHarness = "claude-code";
   254	export type SecondaryHarness = "codex" | "copilot" | "none";
   255	export type ReasoningEffort = "low" | "medium" | "high" | "xhigh";
   256	
   257	export interface RoleAssignment {
   258	  secondary_harness: SecondaryHarness;
   259	  model: string;
   260	  reasoning_effort: ReasoningEffort;
   261	}
   262	
   263	export interface EpicVerifierAssignment extends RoleAssignment {
   264	  label: string;
   265	}
   266	
   267	export interface ImplRunConfig {
   268	  version: 1;
   269	  primary_harness: PrimaryHarness;
   270	  story_implementor: RoleAssignment;
   271	  quick_fixer: RoleAssignment;
   272	  story_verifier_1: RoleAssignment;
   273	  story_verifier_2: RoleAssignment;
   274	  self_review: {
   275	    passes: number;
   276	  };
   277	  epic_verifiers: EpicVerifierAssignment[];
   278	  epic_synthesizer: RoleAssignment;
   279	}
   280	```
   281	
   282	Canonical display labels:
   283	
   284	| Stored Value | Display Label |
   285	|---|---|
   286	| `low` | low |
   287	| `medium` | medium |
   288	| `high` | high |
   289	| `xhigh` | extra high |
   290	
   291	`c12` should be used in explicit-file mode for this feature. The CLI should load either the path passed via `--config` or the default `impl-run.config.json` in the spec-pack root. It should not enable multi-source discovery from home config, `package.json`, or environment overlays in v1. The orchestrator benefits from one obvious config file and one obvious precedence model.
   292	
   293	### Validation Rules
   294	
   295	| Rule | Reason |
   296	|---|---|
   297	| `primary_harness` must be `claude-code` in v1 | v1 always runs inside Claude Code |
   298	| `secondary_harness` must be `codex`, `copilot`, or `none` | keep the contract explicit and narrow |
   299	| `none` means “use primary harness” | avoids extra indirection or derived config fields |
   300	| `story_implementor.secondary_harness !== "copilot"` in v1 | Copilot continuation is not supported for the retained implementor role |
   301	| `self_review.passes` must be `>= 1` and `<= 5` | prevents nonsensical or runaway values |
   302	| `epic_verifiers.length >= 1` | epic verification is mandatory |
   303	| duplicate epic verifier labels are invalid | makes downstream reporting legible |
   304	
   305	### Provider Availability Matrix
   306	
   307	The CLI must validate not just schema shape, but whether the local machine can honor the requested harness assignments.
   308	
   309	```typescript
   310	export interface HarnessAvailability {
   311	  harness: SecondaryHarness | PrimaryHarness;
   312	  available: boolean;
   313	  version?: string;
   314	  authStatus?: "authenticated" | "unknown" | "missing";
   315	  notes: string[];
   316	}
   317	
   318	export interface ProviderMatrix {
   319	  primary: HarnessAvailability;
   320	  secondary: HarnessAvailability[];

    36	
    37	### Critical Mocking Rule
    38	
    39	**Mock only external boundaries.**
    40	
    41	For this feature that means:
    42	
    43	| Boundary | Mock? | Why |
    44	|---|---|---|
    45	| provider CLIs (`claude`, `codex`, `copilot`) | Yes | external process boundary |
    46	| auth / environment availability checks | Yes | external machine state |
    47	| filesystem fixture content | No | spec-pack resolution is core behavior and should run on real temp dirs |
    48	| prompt assembly internals | No | that is feature logic, not a boundary |
    49	| command routing modules | No | command entrypoints should exercise internal orchestration for real |

    51	The command tests should run against real fixture directories on disk and real command handlers. Only process spawning and harness availability should be mocked or faked.

   101	function statusForOutcome(outcome: string): CliStatus {
   102	  switch (outcome) {
   103	    case "ready":
   104	      return "ok";
   105	    case "needs-user-decision":
   106	      return "needs-user-decision";
   107	    case "blocked":
   108	      return "blocked";
   109	    default:
   110	      return "error";
   111	  }
   112	}
   113	
   114	export function exitCodeForStatus(status: CliStatus): number {
   115	  switch (status) {
   116	    case "ok":
   117	      return 0;
   118	    case "needs-user-decision":
   119	      return 2;
   120	    case "blocked":
   121	      return 3;
   122	    case "error":
   123	    default:
   124	      return 1;
   125	  }
   126	}
   127	
   128	export function cliResultEnvelopeSchema<T extends z.ZodTypeAny>(resultSchema: T) {
   129	  return z
   130	    .object({
   131	      command: z.string().min(1),
   132	      version: z.literal(1),
   133	      status: cliStatusSchema,
   134	      outcome: z.string().min(1),
   135	      result: resultSchema.optional(),
   136	      errors: z.array(cliErrorSchema),
   137	      warnings: z.array(z.string()),
   138	      artifacts: z.array(cliArtifactRefSchema),
   139	      startedAt: z.string().min(1),
   140	      finishedAt: z.string().min(1),
   141	    })
   142	    .superRefine((value, ctx) => {
   143	      const expectedStatus = statusForOutcome(value.outcome);
   144	      if (expectedStatus !== value.status) {
   145	        ctx.addIssue({
   146	          code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
   147	          message: `Envelope status '${value.status}' does not match outcome '${value.outcome}'`,
   148	          path: ["status"],
   149	        });
   150	      }
   151	    });
   152	}
   153	
   154	export function createResultEnvelope<T>(input: {
   155	  command: string;
   156	  outcome: string;
   157	  result?: T;
   158	  errors?: CliError[];
   159	  warnings?: string[];
   160	  artifacts?: CliArtifactRef[];
   161	  startedAt: string;
   162	  finishedAt: string;
   163	}) {
   164	  return {
   165	    command: input.command,
   166	    version: 1 as const,
   167	    status: statusForOutcome(input.outcome),
   168	    outcome: input.outcome,
   169	    result: input.result,
   170	    errors: input.errors ?? [],
   171	    warnings: input.warnings ?? [],
   172	    artifacts: input.artifacts ?? [],
   173	    startedAt: input.startedAt,
   174	    finishedAt: input.finishedAt,
   175	  };
   176	}

    19	function extractStoryTitle(content: string, fallback: string): string {
    20	  const match = content.match(/^#\s+(.+)$/m);
    21	  return match?.[1]?.trim() || fallback;
    22	}
    23	
    24	function numericPrefix(fileName: string): number | null {
    25	  const match = fileName.match(/^(\d+)-/);
    26	  return match ? Number(match[1]) : null;
    27	}

    29	export async function resolveStoryOrder(
    30	  storiesDir: string
    31	): Promise<StoryOrderResolution> {
    32	  const entries = await readdir(storiesDir, { withFileTypes: true });
    33	  const fileNames = entries
    34	    .filter((entry) => entry.isFile() && entry.name.endsWith(".md"))
    35	    .map((entry) => entry.name)
    36	    .filter((fileName) => fileName !== "coverage.md");
    37	  const numericFileNames = fileNames.filter(
    38	    (fileName) => numericPrefix(fileName) !== null
    39	  );
    40	  const nonNumericFileNames = fileNames.filter(
    41	    (fileName) => numericPrefix(fileName) === null
    42	  );
    43	
    44	  if (numericFileNames.length > 0 && nonNumericFileNames.length > 0) {
    45	    return {
    46	      status: "needs-user-decision",
    47	      stories: [],
    48	      notes: [
    49	        "Mixed numeric and non-numeric story filenames require an explicit user ordering decision.",
    50	      ],
    51	    };
    52	  }
    53	
    54	  const notes: string[] = [];
    55	  const orderedFileNames =
    56	    numericFileNames.length === 0
    57	      ? [...fileNames].sort((left, right) => left.localeCompare(right))
    58	      : [...fileNames].sort((left, right) => {
    59	          const leftPrefix = numericPrefix(left) ?? Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER;
    60	          const rightPrefix = numericPrefix(right) ?? Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER;
    61	          if (leftPrefix !== rightPrefix) {
    62	            return leftPrefix - rightPrefix;
    63	          }
    64	          return left.localeCompare(right);
    65	        });
    66	
    67	  if (numericFileNames.length === 0 && fileNames.length > 0) {
    68	    notes.push(
    69	      "Story filenames do not use numeric prefixes; lexical ordering was applied."
    70	    );
    71	  }
    72	
    73	  const stories: StoryRecord[] = [];
    74	  for (const [index, fileName] of orderedFileNames.entries()) {
    75	    const path = join(storiesDir, fileName);
    76	    const content = await readTextFile(path);
    77	    stories.push({
    78	      id: basename(fileName, ".md"),
    79	      title: extractStoryTitle(content, basename(fileName, ".md")),
    80	      path,
    81	      order: index + 1,
    82	    });
    83	  }
    84	
    85	  return {
    86	    status: "ready",
    87	    stories,
    88	    notes,
    89	  };
    90	}

    88	async function checkHarnessAvailability(input: {

    90	  cwd: string;
    91	  env?: Record<string, string | undefined>;
    92	  timeoutMs: number;
    93	}): Promise<HarnessAvailability> {
    94	  const executable = executableForHarness(input.harness);
    95	  if (!executable) {
    96	    return {
    97	      harness: "none",
    98	      available: true,
    99	      authStatus: "unknown",
   100	      notes: [],
   101	    };
   102	  }
   103	
   104	  const version = await runCommand({
   105	    file: executable,
   106	    args: ["--version"],
   107	    cwd: input.cwd,
   108	    env: input.env,
   109	    timeoutMs: input.timeoutMs,
   110	  });
   111	  if (!version.success) {
   112	    return {
   113	      harness: input.harness,
   114	      available: false,
   115	      authStatus: version.code === "ENOENT" ? "missing" : "unknown",
   116	      notes: failureNotes(version, executable, "--version"),
   117	    };
   118	  }
   119	
   120	  const auth = await runCommand({
   121	    file: executable,
   122	    args: ["auth", "status"],
   123	    cwd: input.cwd,
   124	    env: input.env,
   125	    timeoutMs: input.timeoutMs,
   126	  });
   127	  if (!auth.success) {
   128	    const authStatus =
   129	      auth.timedOut || auth.code === "ETIMEDOUT"
   130	        ? "unknown"
   131	        : auth.code === 1 || auth.stderr.toLowerCase().includes("missing")
   132	          ? "missing"
   133	          : "unknown";
   134	
   135	    return {
   136	      harness: input.harness,
   137	      available: false,
   138	      version: version.stdout,
   139	      authStatus,
   140	      notes: failureNotes(auth, executable, "auth status"),
   141	    };
   142	  }
   143	
   144	  return {
   145	    harness: input.harness,
   146	    available: true,
   147	    version: version.stdout,
   148	    authStatus: "authenticated",
   149	    notes: auth.stdout ? [auth.stdout] : [],
   150	  };
   151	}

   153	function requestedSecondaryHarnesses(config: ImplRunConfig): SecondaryHarness[] {
   154	  const harnesses = new Set<SecondaryHarness>();
   155	  for (const assignment of [
   156	    config.story_implementor,
   157	    config.quick_fixer,
   158	    config.story_verifier_1,
   159	    config.story_verifier_2,
   160	    ...config.epic_verifiers,
   161	    config.epic_synthesizer,
   162	  ]) {
   163	    if (assignment.secondary_harness !== "none") {
   164	      harnesses.add(assignment.secondary_harness);
   165	    }
   166	  }
   167	  return [...harnesses];
   168	}
   169	
   170	export async function resolveProviderMatrix(input: {
   171	  specPackRoot: string;
   172	  config: ImplRunConfig;
   173	  env?: Record<string, string | undefined>;
   174	  timeoutMs?: number;
   175	}): Promise<ProviderMatrix> {
   176	  const cwd = (await resolveGitRepoRoot(input.specPackRoot)) ?? input.specPackRoot;
   177	  const timeoutMs = input.timeoutMs ?? DEFAULT_PROVIDER_CHECK_TIMEOUT_MS;
   178	  const primary = await checkHarnessAvailability({
   179	    harness: "claude-code",
   180	    cwd,
   181	    env: input.env,
   182	    timeoutMs,
   183	  });
   184	  const secondary: HarnessAvailability[] = [];
   185	
   186	  for (const harness of requestedSecondaryHarnesses(input.config)) {
   187	    secondary.push(
   188	      await checkHarnessAvailability({
   189	        harness,
   190	        cwd,
   191	        env: input.env,
   192	        timeoutMs,
   193	      })
   194	    );
   195	  }
   196	
   197	  return {
   198	    primary,
   199	    secondary,
   200	  };
   201	}

    95	export default defineCommand({
    96	  meta: {
    97	    name: "preflight",
    98	    description:
    99	      "Validate run config, verification gates, and provider availability.",
   100	  },
   101	  args: {
   102	    "spec-pack-root": {
   103	      type: "string",
   104	      description: "Absolute or relative path to the spec-pack root",
   105	      required: true,
   106	    },
   107	    config: {
   108	      type: "string",
   109	      description: "Explicit run-config file relative to the spec-pack root",
   110	    },
   111	    "story-gate": {
   112	      type: "string",
   113	      description: "Explicit story verification gate command",
   114	    },
   115	    "epic-gate": {
   116	      type: "string",
   117	      description: "Explicit epic verification gate command",
   118	    },
   119	    json: {
   120	      type: "boolean",
   121	      description: "Emit the structured JSON envelope on stdout",
   122	    },
   123	  },
   124	  async run({ args }) {
   125	    const startedAt = new Date().toISOString();
   126	
   127	    try {
   128	      const inspectResult = await inspectSpecPack(args["spec-pack-root"]);
   129	      const artifactPath = await nextArtifactPath(
   130	        inspectResult.specPackRoot,
   131	        "preflight"
   132	      );
   133	
   134	      if (inspectResult.status !== "ready") {
   135	        const envelope: OutputEnvelope = createResultEnvelope({
   136	          command: "preflight",
   137	          outcome: inspectResult.status,
   138	          errors: [
   139	            {
   140	              code: "INVALID_SPEC_PACK",
   141	              message:
   142	                inspectResult.status === "blocked"
   143	                  ? "Spec-pack inspection failed"
   144	                  : "Spec-pack inspection requires a user decision",
   145	              detail:
   146	                inspectResult.blockers.join("; ") || inspectResult.notes.join("; "),
   147	            },
   148	          ],
   149	          artifacts: [
   150	            {
   151	              kind: "result-envelope",
   152	              path: artifactPath,
   153	            },
   154	          ],
   155	          startedAt,
   156	          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
   157	        });
   158	
   159	        await writeJsonArtifact(artifactPath, envelope);
   160	        emitOutput({
   161	          envelope,
   162	          json: Boolean(args.json),
   163	        });
   164	        process.exitCode = exitCodeForStatus(envelope.status);
   165	        return;
   166	      }
   167	
   168	      const validatedConfig = await loadRunConfig({
   169	        specPackRoot: inspectResult.specPackRoot,
   170	        configPath: args.config,
   171	      });
   172	      const providerMatrix = await resolveProviderMatrix({
   173	        specPackRoot: inspectResult.specPackRoot,
   174	        config: validatedConfig,
   175	        env: process.env,
   176	      });
   177	      const gateResolution = await resolveVerificationGates({
   178	        specPackRoot: inspectResult.specPackRoot,
   179	        explicitStoryGate: args["story-gate"],
   180	        explicitEpicGate: args["epic-gate"],
   181	      });
   182	      const notes = [...inspectResult.notes];
   183	
   184	      if (
   185	        [
   186	          validatedConfig.story_implementor,
   187	          validatedConfig.quick_fixer,
   188	          validatedConfig.story_verifier_1,
   189	          validatedConfig.story_verifier_2,
   190	          ...validatedConfig.epic_verifiers,
   191	          validatedConfig.epic_synthesizer,
   192	        ].every((assignment) => assignment.secondary_harness === "none")
   193	      ) {
   194	        notes.push(
   195	          "GPT-capable secondary harnesses are unavailable for this run; the orchestrator should record the Claude-only degraded mode."
   196	        );
   197	      }
   198	
   199	      const providerErrors = unavailableProviderErrors(providerMatrix);
   200	      const blockers: string[] = [];
   201	      const errors: CliError[] = [];
   202	      let outcome: "ready" | "needs-user-decision" | "blocked" = "ready";
   203	
   204	      if (providerErrors.length > 0) {
   205	        outcome = "blocked";
   206	        errors.push(...providerErrors);
   207	        blockers.push(...providerErrors.map((error) => error.message));
   208	      } else if (gateResolution.status === "needs-user-decision") {
   209	        outcome = "needs-user-decision";
   210	        errors.push(...gateResolution.errors);
   211	        notes.push(...gateResolution.notes);
   212	      }
   213	
   214	      const preflightResult: PreflightResult = {
   215	        status: outcome,
   216	        validatedConfig,
   217	        providerMatrix,
   218	        verificationGates: gateResolution.verificationGates,
   219	        configValidationNotes: [],

   222	          snippetsReady: true,
   223	          notes: [],
   224	        },

   226	        notes,
   227	      };
   228	      const envelope = cliResultEnvelopeSchema(preflightResultSchema).parse(
   229	        createResultEnvelope({
   230	          command: "preflight",
   231	          outcome,
   232	          result: preflightResult,
   233	          errors,
   234	          artifacts: [
   235	            {
   236	              kind: "result-envelope",
   237	              path: artifactPath,
   238	            },
   239	          ],
   240	          startedAt,
   241	          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
   242	        })
   243	      );
   244	
   245	      await writeJsonArtifact(artifactPath, envelope);
   246	      emitOutput({
   247	        envelope,
   248	        json: Boolean(args.json),
   249	      });
   250	      process.exitCode = exitCodeForStatus(envelope.status);

   262	          errors: [

   267	            },
   268	          ],

   271	        });
   272	
   273	        emitOutput({
   274	          envelope,
   275	          json: Boolean(args.json),
   276	        });
   277	        process.exitCode = exitCodeForStatus(envelope.status);
   278	        return;
   279	      }
   280	
   281	      const envelope: OutputEnvelope = createResultEnvelope({
   282	        command: "preflight",
   283	        outcome: "error",
   284	        errors: [
   285	          {
   286	            code: "UNEXPECTED_ERROR",
   287	            message: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error),
   288	          },
   289	        ],
   290	        startedAt,
   291	        finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
   292	      });
   293	
   294	      emitOutput({

   296	        json: Boolean(args.json),
   297	      });
   298	      process.exitCode = exitCodeForStatus(envelope.status);
   299	    }

   301	});

docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-01/006-verifier-codex53-high.jsonl:32:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_15","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba /Users/leemoore/code/liminal-spec/src/phases/claude-impl.md | sed -n '1,340p'"","aggregated_output":"     1	# Claude Implementation
     2	
     3	Use `ls-claude-impl` when a completed spec pack already exists and Claude Code needs a disciplined implementation workflow with a bundled bounded-operation CLI.
     4	
     5	## Startup Orientation
     6	
     7	Resolve the spec-pack root before reading deeply. The minimum v1 layout is:
     8	
     9	- `epic.md`
    10	- `tech-design.md`
    11	- `test-plan.md`
    12	- `stories/`
    13	
    14	Run `bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs inspect --spec-pack-root <path> --json` as the first runtime check. Capture the returned tech-design shape, required artifacts, story inventory, and insert status before you move on to run configuration or provider-backed work.
    15	
    16	Read every story file in `stories/` in order before starting Story 0 or Story 1. Do not begin implementation from a partial story read.
    17	
    18	## Initialization Steps
    19	
    20	1. Confirm the spec-pack root and run `inspect`.
    21	2. If `team-impl-log.md` is absent, create `team-impl-log.md` and initialize the run in `SETUP`.
    22	3. If `team-impl-log.md` already exists, read it first and resume from the recorded state.
    23	4. Record whether the spec pack is a two-file or four-file tech-design layout.
    24	5. Record whether `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` and `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` are present. Their absence is non-blocking.
    25	6. Discover verification gates in this precedence order: explicit CLI flags, `impl-run.config.json` entries if the run uses them later, package scripts, project policy docs, then CI configuration.
    26	7. If the policy stays ambiguous after that precedence order, pause for a user decision instead of improvising.
    27	8. Run `preflight` before provider-backed work.
    28	9. After `preflight`, record the validated run configuration, available providers, active role defaults, verification gates, and degraded fallback conditions in `team-impl-log.md`.
    29	
    30	## Default Resolution Contract
    31	
    32	- Claude Code is the primary harness for every run.
    33	- If Codex CLI is available, default the story implementor to Codex `gpt-5.4` with high reasoning.
    34	- If Codex CLI is available, default the verifier pair to Codex `gpt-5.4` with extra high reasoning plus Claude Sonnet with high reasoning.
    35	- If Codex CLI is available, default epic verifier GPT lanes and the epic synthesizer to Codex `gpt-5.4` with extra high reasoning, paired with a Claude Sonnet verifier lane.
    36	- If Codex is unavailable but Copilot CLI is available, use Copilot for the fresh-session GPT roles and keep the retained story implementor on Claude Sonnet high.
    37	- If no GPT-capable secondary harness is available, switch the run to Claude-only defaults, record the degraded-diversity condition explicitly, and note that verifier diversity is weaker in this run.
    38	
    39	## State Ownership
    40	
    41	- `impl-run.config.json` is orchestrator-owned configuration.
    42	- `team-impl-log.md` is the durable run record.
    43	- The CLI performs one bounded operation per call and returns structured results.
    44	- The CLI does not own story progression, acceptance, or recovery strategy between calls.
    45	
    46	## Read Next
    47	
    48	After the initial `inspect` pass and log setup, read:
    49	
    50	1. `references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md`
    51	2. `references/claude-impl-process-playbook.md`
    52	3. `references/claude-impl-cli-operations.md`
    53	4. `references/claude-impl-prompt-system.md`

   101	export interface CliRunResult {
   102	  exitCode: number;
   103	  stdout: string;
   104	  stderr: string;
   105	}
   106	
   107	export async function runSourceCli(
   108	  args: string[],
   109	  options: {
   110	    env?: Record<string, string | undefined>;
   111	  } = {}
   112	): Promise<CliRunResult> {
   113	  const proc = Bun.spawn(["bun", "run", "processes/impl-cli/cli.ts", ...args], {
   114	    cwd: ROOT,
   115	    stdout: "pipe",
   116	    stderr: "pipe",
   117	    env: {
   118	      ...process.env,
   119	      ...options.env,
   120	      FORCE_COLOR: "0",
   121	    },
   122	  });
   123	
   124	  const stdout = await new Response(proc.stdout).text();
   125	  const stderr = await new Response(proc.stderr).text();
   126	  const exitCode = await proc.exited;
   127	
   128	  return {
   129	    exitCode,
   130	    stdout,
   131	    stderr,
   132	  };
   133	}

   135	export function parseJsonOutput<T>(stdout: string): T {
   136	  return JSON.parse(stdout.trim()) as T;
   137	}
